there is a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer, and satisfies the inequality:

$$
\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}
$$

where $ \{x\} $ denotes the fractional part of $ x $. this inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of powers of $ x $, starting from $ x $ and going up to $ x^{99} $, with the exponents all being odd numbers. the fractional part of a number is defined as the difference between the number and its integer part, so $ \{x\} = x - \lfloor x \rfloor $, where $ \lfloor x \rfloor $ is the greatest integer less than or equal to $ x $.

this means that each term in the sum is between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). the sum of these terms is less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $, which is a very small number. this suggests that the fractional parts of the powers of $ x $ must be very small, and their sum must be extremely small.

since $ x > 1 $, the powers $ x^1, x^3, x^5, \ldots, x^{99} $ will grow rapidly. the fractional part of each power depends on the fractional part of $ x $, which is a value between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). if the fractional part of $ x $ is very small, then the fractional parts of the higher powers of $ x $ will also be very small, because multiplying by $ x $ (which is greater than 1) increases the value, and the fractional part is determined by the remainder after dividing by the integer part.
